University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 14 August 2019 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810  
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT 

Summary/Diagnoses: History of seizures following travel abroad; traumatic brain injury many years prior with residual mild cognitive issues; improving sleep pattern; osteoporosis under primary care follow‑up; previous alcohol dependence now in sustained remission; current smoker. 

Medication:  
- Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily  
- Carbamazepine (modified release) 400 mg twice daily  
- Topiramate 50 mg morning and 100 mg evening  
- Thiamine, Vitamin B compound, Calcium/Vitamin D supplements, Atorvastatin, Lansoprazole, Bisoprolol, Ramipril, Carbocisteine, Emollients as required 

I reviewed Bill in clinic today accompanied by his key worker, who has supported him for the past year. He reports a notable improvement in day-to-day steadiness and confidence. He is eating regularly and has resumed short walks to the local shops. On assessment, gait was slow but stable without aids, and he rose from the chair independently. There is no focal joint pain; tone is slightly increased on the right but non-painful through range. 

Seizure history and current status: His initial event was in April 2019 in Germany, arising from sleep. He awoke with jerking of the left arm and facial twitching. A second event occurred in Italy the following July 2019, once more during the night, lasting four minutes with similar semiology. Since returning to the UK, there have been no further events reported. He and his key worker attribute improvement to better sleep routines—bedtime and wake time are consistent, and caffeine is avoided after midday. Sleep hygiene improved; pattern appears more stable. No episodes of confusion on waking, no witnessed nocturnal movements, and no injuries. 

Examination: Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations were unremarkable. Neurologically, speech was clear, cranial nerves intact, mild right-sided pyramidal pattern of tone without weakness, coordination preserved, and sensation grossly intact. Cognitive screening was unchanged from prior review. 

Investigations: He has had routine bloods arranged by the GP last month (FBC, U&Es, LFTs, bone profile, vitamin D) reportedly within acceptable limits; we will request the printout for records. He has not yet completed the community sleep assessment referral but feels current measures are helping. No recent imaging required given stable course. 

Plan:  
- Continue current antiepileptic regimen without change.  
- Reinforce sleep hygiene strategies, which appear to be contributing to stability.  
- Obtain recent blood results from practice and repeat only if clinically indicated.  
- Safety advice reviewed for nocturnal events and travel; he understands to seek review if events recur or change in character.  
- Follow-up in 6 months, sooner if further episodes occur. 

Overall, I am encouraged by the trajectory; he is clinically stable and functionally improving, with no further events since July 2019. 

Yours sincerely

St Mary's Hospital 

Institute of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Ahmed  
Riverside Medical Practice  
12 Wharf Street, London SE1 3AB  

Dear Dr Ahmed,  

Daniel Morton, DOB: 04-11-1991, Hospital No: NHS773520 NHS No. 9081763452  
Flat 3, 29 Forge Court, London, SE16 2LP 

Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1 g twice daily. He reports good adherence and tolerates this without notable side effects. 

Since his last review, he continues working in metal fabrication and welding. He uses an auto-darkening visor and follows workplace safety protocols. Over the past six weeks he has noticed that brief episodes are tending to bunch together after he wakes, occurring on several mornings each week despite taking his tablets as prescribed. He describes these as short spells of disorientation with a metallic taste and a need to pause for 30–60 seconds, without loss of awareness, sometimes repeating two or three times within the same morning. He has not had any injuries or prolonged events. There is no clear association with missed doses, alcohol, illness, or sleep deprivation. He wonders whether intermittent exposure to arc light is relevant; he keeps the visor down consistently but occasionally steps into the welding bay where others are working. 

We discussed practical measures around work. He will continue visor use and is arranging with his supervisor to minimise exposure to simultaneous welding by colleagues at close range, and to take scheduled breaks if he experiences prodromal symptoms. He does not drive. 

For the moment I have not altered his medication. If these morning groupings become more frequent or start to impact safety at work, we could consider a cautious increase of levetiracetam to 1.25 g twice daily, or the addition of a second agent such as lamotrigine after discussion of risks and benefits. He has been given a seizure diary template to document timing, potential triggers (including arc-light exposure, sleep patterns, and stress), and any associated features. 

Please could you check routine bloods (FBC, U&E, LFTs) at your convenience and let us know if there are any abnormalities. He is otherwise well, with stable mood and no cognitive concerns. 

We will review him again in four months, sooner if the morning episodes escalate or if there are any safety concerns at work. 

Yours sincerely

Epilepsy Centre 

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 08 March 2018 

Dr Patel  
Southwark Neurology Service  
Guy Street Health Centre, 12 Guy Street, London SE1 3RT  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Michael Harris, DOB: 14-07-1990, Hospital No: A9374621 NHS No. 7402195368  
Flat 3, 28 Mill Wharf, London, SE16 4LP  

Diagnoses: 

Seizure disorder under investigation. 

Thank you for asking me to review Michael Harris, who works as a warehouse picker in an environment with tall racking and narrow aisles, with variable lifting demands and shift-dependent pace. He attended today with his partner. He describes brief episodes of unresponsiveness with behavioural arrest, sometimes preceded by a rising internal sensation, occurring at work and at home. Episodes last under a minute with rapid recovery, though he reports post-event fatigue. There have been no witnessed convulsions. He denies tongue biting or incontinence. There is no clear diurnal pattern, but he notes that night shifts and faster-paced picking runs seem to correspond with more events. No alcohol excess. Sleep may be curtailed on rotation weeks. 

Electrographic seizures frequent on EEG (~4/h) were recorded during a recent ambulatory study, correlating with subtle clinical pauses observed by the technician and by his partner on diary review. MRI brain (epilepsy protocol) is reported as normal. Routine bloods including U&E, LFT, glucose and B12 were within normal limits. 

Current medication: none for seizures. He uses ibuprofen intermittently for musculoskeletal strain related to lifting tasks. No known drug allergies. 

Examination today was unremarkable with normal neurological findings. Cardiovascular and postural vitals were stable. 

Assessment: Recurrent paroxysmal episodes with captured electrographic seizures on EEG as above. The semiology is non-specific and does not allow classification at this stage. No structural lesion identified on MRI. Occupational setting involves potential hazards (working at height between racking, narrow aisles, variable lifting). Patient reports avoiding ladders and high platforms since the episodes began, and his supervisor has temporarily allocated him to ground-level picking only. 

Plan: 
- Initiate levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily, with counselling regarding mood and irritability; patient given contact numbers for adverse effect reporting. 
- Provide seizure first-aid information to patient and partner; he will also share this with his line manager. 
- Arrange formal occupational health review to document current duties and agree interim risk controls at work. 
- Repeat ambulatory EEG after 8–12 weeks on treatment to gauge electrographic response, given the previously observed frequency (~4/h). 
- Safety advice discussed including bathing/showering precautions and machinery awareness; patient already self-restricting higher-risk tasks at work. 
- DVLA guidance discussed and leaflet provided. 
- Review in clinic in three months, sooner if escalation or adverse effects. 

Please let me know if there are any queries or additional occupational documents you would like us to complete. 

Yours sincerely

Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 14 May 2016 

Dr Amelia Khan  
St George’s University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust  
Department of Neurology, Blackshaw Road, London SW17 0QT  

Dear Dr Amelia Khan,  

Emily Carter, DOB: 14-03-1992, Hospital No: H392847 NHS No. 9472635811  
22 Brookfield Avenue, London, SW16 3QP 

Diagnoses: 

Recurrent unprovoked seizures under investigation 
Co-existing anxiety with intermittent low mood 

Current Medication: 

Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily  
Sertraline 50 mg once daily 

Plan: 

- Continue Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily; no dose change today  
- Safety counselling reinforced (sleep hygiene, alcohol moderation, adherence)  
- Bloods: U&E, LFTs, FBC, Vitamin D today  
- MRI brain with epilepsy protocol already requested; await appointment  
- Routine EEG booked; consider ambulatory EEG if events remain unclear  
- Continue Sertraline 50 mg daily; liaise with GP for ongoing mental health review and consider IAPT referral  
- Provide seizure diary to track timing, duration, potential triggers, and post-event features  
- DVLA guidance discussed; patient advised regarding driving restrictions as per current regulations  
- Follow-up neurology clinic in 4 months, earlier if clusters escalate or medication intolerance occurs 

I reviewed Emily Carter in clinic today following three months of intermittent brief events. They describe stereotyped episodes with a sudden sense of "switching off" and loss of awareness for 10–20 seconds, sometimes followed by a short period of confusion. There are no consistent focal auras reported. Injuries have not occurred. Family have witnessed two events and noted blank staring with unresponsiveness, without clear limb jerking. 

Regarding frequency, the patient reports that episodes tend to come in small runs: typically around four to six short spells grouped together on days when they occur, most commonly later in the day towards the evening period. There may then be several days without events. Sleep disruption and higher anxiety levels appear to precede these clusters, and caffeine excess may contribute. Alcohol intake is modest and there is no recreational drug use. 

Past medical history is notable for longstanding anxiety with intermittent low mood, currently improved on Sertraline 50 mg daily for the past six months, with no reported side effects. Screening today suggests stable mood; no suicidal ideation. They are open to psychological therapies and will discuss an IAPT referral with you. 

On examination today, neurological assessment was unremarkable. Routine blood tests earlier this year were normal. We have arranged updated baseline bloods today. An MRI brain with epilepsy protocol has been requested, and a routine EEG has been scheduled; should events remain poorly captured, we would consider ambulatory monitoring. 

Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily was started by the emergency department six weeks ago after two clusters within a fortnight. Since initiation, the patient reports a reduction in event severity and number per cluster, with no new adverse effects beyond mild initial fatigue which has settled. We discussed adherence, common triggers, and the importance of sleep regularity. A seizure diary was provided to document timing (particularly evening tendency), precipitants, and recovery features to better guide further management. 

We reviewed DVLA guidance and driving restrictions; the patient understands current requirements and will inform the DVLA as appropriate. 

I will see Emily Carter again in approximately four months to review the imaging, EEG results, and diary data. Please feel free to contact me sooner if there is any clinical concern, a significant increase in clusters, or medication intolerance. 

With best wishes,

Epilepsy Centre

Neurosciences Division

From: Dr Alice Morgan (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Date: 11 March 2022
To: neurology.team@nhs.net
Cc: Dr John Williams (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST); Dr Sarah Patel (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Subject: Review and medication plan

Dear Alice,

Diagnosis:
The patient has generalised epilepsy.

Background and current status:
They work on the gate line at a busy metro station where prolonged standing, variable sleep patterns due to early shifts, and occasional crowd surges are part of the daily environment. They report being mindful of hydration and breaks, and have avoided known precipitants where possible. No further tonic-clonic seizures have occurred since 12/2020, although two to three single jerks remain, typically noted at the end of long shifts or on waking after reduced sleep. There is no associated loss of awareness with these jerks and no injuries reported. 

Current medication:
1. Tegretol Retard 600 mg twice daily
2. Keppra 500 mg twice daily

Assessment:
Overall control of tonic-clonic events is maintained. The residual brief myoclonic jerks are infrequent and appear situational. Mood and cognition are stable on the current regimen. There have been no reported adverse effects beyond mild tiredness after late finishes.

Plan:
Given the sustained absence of tonic-clonic seizures and the limited impact of occasional single jerks, we will trial a cautious introduction of Lacosamide to optimise control while monitoring tolerability in the context of physically demanding work.

Titration schedule for Lacosamide:
- Week 1: 25 mg at night
- Week 2: 25 mg twice daily
- Week 3: 25 mg in the morning and 50 mg at night
- Week 4: 50 mg twice daily
Then continue 50 mg twice daily for four months until review.

No immediate changes to Tegretol Retard or Keppra during the above titration. If Lacosamide is well tolerated and jerks reduce further without impact on alertness at work, we will consider tapering and stopping Keppra at the next clinic visit. The patient has been advised to maintain consistent sleep where possible, keep adequate hydration during shifts, and report any breakthrough events, side effects, or issues with balance or vision promptly.

Follow-up:
A review appointment has been arranged in four months.

With best wishes,

Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 21 March 2014 

Dr Fiona  
Centre for Epilepsy  
Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Fiona,                                                                                                         

Thomas Reed, DOB: 09-11-1984, Hospital No: H726391 NHS No. 9042175638  
Flat 3, 28 Kingfisher Road, London, SE15 4DA  

Epilepsy 

Medication: Lamotrigine titrated since early February 2014 

Current regimen: Lamotrigine dispersible 50mg in the morning and 75mg in the evening (patient-reported adherence; uses pill organiser) 

Lamotrigine Blood Level: 

10 March 2014: 3.4 mg/L (reference range 1.0 to 15.0 mg/L). Dose at time of sampling: 50mg AM / 75mg PM 

Seizures: Following two episodes of dizziness and subsequent headaches, his symptoms improved with supportive care. On 18 February the absences diminished after dose escalation. His last episode was recorded on 26 February and he has remained well since. Considering that he has now been free of seizures for three weeks and his overall condition is stable, no immediate action is necessary. 

I reviewed Thomas Reed today in clinic. He works as a warehouse picker operating between tall racking and narrow aisles, where lifting tasks and walking pace vary by shift. He reports that the earlier dizziness occurred at the end of a busy late shift and that he took breaks with oral fluids and sat out; there was no loss of awareness at work reported by colleagues. Since the lamotrigine adjustment on 18 February, he notes steadier concentration, improved sleep hygiene, and no further events since 26 February. No new neurological deficits, no morning myoclonus, and no tongue biting reported. He has not driven. 

Observations today: BP 124/76, pulse 72 regular, neurological examination unremarkable. No rash or gastrointestinal intolerance to lamotrigine. Weight stable. 

Plan: 
- Continue lamotrigine 50mg AM / 75mg PM. 
- Safety: maintain regular breaks, hydration, and prompt reporting of any recurrent episodes at work (particularly around lifting or working at height). 
- Blood level recheck if clinical change or dose adjustment needed; otherwise routine review in three months. 
- No immediate action is necessary given three weeks event-free and overall stability. Patient has contact details for rapid access if symptoms recur. 

Yours sincerely

KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Jonathan Hale
Riverside Health Centre

12 Brook Lane, Southwark
London SE1 9ZZ
Dear Dr Jonathan Hale
Alex Morgan, DOB: 14-03-1987, Hospital No: H927451 NHS No. 8427193056
Flat 3, 28 Willow Road, London NW3 1TR

Diagnoses/summary: Drug-resistant focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe origin. Photosensitivity to flicker and bright light reported. Prior EEGs and video-EEG have demonstrated frequent focal discharges. Electrographic seizures frequent on EEG (~6/h).

MRI brain (2018): No definite focal cortical dysplasia or hippocampal sclerosis identified. Subtle asymmetry of the left hippocampus noted without signal change (radiology felt non-diagnostic).

Previous anti-seizure medications: carbamazepine (partial response, dizziness), levetiracetam (mood irritability), valproate (weight gain, tremor), lamotrigine (rash at higher dose), lacosamide (nausea), clobazam PRN (sedation), perampanel (behavioural change), zonisamide (poor appetite), topiramate (word-finding difficulty), gabapentin (ineffective).

Not previously tried: brivaracetam, cenobamate, eslicarbazepine, pregabalin, rufinamide, tiagabine, oxcarbazepine.

Comorbidities: Migraine without aura; mild generalised anxiety (patient-reported). No known cardiac history. Routine bloods last month within normal limits.

I reviewed Alex Morgan in clinic today. They attended alone. They described focal aware episodes with epigastric rising sensation and brief speech arrest, lasting 20–40 seconds, sometimes progressing to impaired awareness with automatisms. Triggers include flickering lights in supermarkets, sunlight through trees while travelling in a car, and certain video content. They have been avoiding known triggers where possible and now use tinted lenses outdoors. Sleep deprivation also appears to lower their threshold.

Since the last review, they report variable days with clusters, particularly late afternoon. A recent ambulatory EEG performed via our service recorded frequent focal discharges over the left temporal region with evolution in rhythm and morphology consistent with electrographic seizures frequent on EEG (~6/h). The patient noted subtle subjective correlates with only a proportion of these events, aligning with under-recognised subclinical activity.

Current medication:
- Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily (maintained due to prior rash at higher dose)
- Clobazam 10 mg nocte PRN up to four nights/week
- Riboflavin 400 mg daily (self-initiated for migraine)

Allergies/adverse reactions: lamotrigine rash at ≥150 mg BD previously; levetiracetam-related mood change.

Plan:
- We discussed therapeutic options including optimisation with brivaracetam or eslicarbazepine, and the possibility of cenobamate in future if standard options fail. We also discussed non-pharmacological strategies given photosensitivity (continued trigger avoidance, screen settings with reduced brightness/blue light filters, and steady ambient lighting). The patient prefers to consider medication changes after a further discussion with family.
- I have arranged an epilepsy nurse specialist telephone review in 4–6 weeks to revisit adding brivaracetam, and to reinforce safety advice around photosensitive triggers. Seizure diary to continue, noting lighting context and sleep pattern.
- We will request repeat MRI with epilepsy protocol given persistent focal features and frequent electrographic seizures, and will discuss at the next MDT for potential surgical candidacy if imaging or semiology evolves.
- Driving: We reiterated DVLA guidance relevant to ongoing focal seizures; the patient verbalised understanding.

Follow-up: Routine clinic in 3 months, sooner if deterioration or if they wish to proceed earlier with medication change.

Yours sincerely

Hi Priya

Epilepsy Diagnosis:

Focal epilepsy


Present Medication:

1. Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily
2. Brivaracetam 100 mg twice daily


Present Seizure Frequency:

The patient keeps a paper seizure calendar and brings the monthly logs to each appointment. Over the last eight weeks, simple partial seizures happen after lack of sleep, typically one to two events in the morning following nights with less than five hours of rest. No secondary generalisation has been reported in this period. Aura remains consistent with brief right temporal tingling and a rising epigastric sensation lasting 20–40 seconds without loss of awareness.


Plan of Action:

We reviewed the paper logs together and the pattern of sleep deprivation preceding events is clear. We discussed common lifestyle precipitants and the patient will continue using the paper calendar to track bedtime, wake time, caffeine, and shift patterns alongside seizure occurrences. No immediate drug changes are planned today given overall stability and tolerability of current therapy. We agreed to:
- Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily and brivaracetam 100 mg twice daily as currently prescribed.
- Maintain a regular sleep schedule aiming for 7–8 hours per night and record any deviations on the calendar.
- Bring the updated paper logs to the next review so we can correlate events with sleep duration more precisely.

I have arranged a follow‑up appointment in three months. If the frequency increases or if there is any impairment of awareness, injury, or progression to bilateral tonic‑clonic seizures, the patient will contact the clinic sooner via the neurology advice line so we can consider adjustments.

With best wishes

KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 17 December 2023

Dr Jonathan Hale
South Quay Health Centre

12 Mill Quay, Deptford, London SE8 3FU
Dear Dr Jonathan Hale
Thomas Reid, DOB: 14-03-1996, Hospital No: R839274 NHS No. 7410926653
Flat 3, 27 Brookmill Road, London SE8 4JN 

Diagnoses/summary: Focal epilepsy, drug-resistant, with generalised tonic–clonic seizures, absences, focal non-motor events with disorientation, and drop attacks. Mood and sleep disturbance in the context of examination and professional assessment stress; reported deterioration in overall day-to-day functioning and resilience.

Imaging and investigations: Previous MRI reported as non-lesional. Prior routine EEGs with intermittent focal slowing; no recent video-EEG telemetry. No new structural risk factors reported.

Previous and current anti-seizure medications: History includes levetiracetam, carbamazepine, valproate, phenytoin, phenobarbital, gabapentin, clobazam, lacosamide, and zonisamide. Currently taking lamotrigine and perampanel; adherence reported, though variable timing noted during recent exam period. No known medication allergies.

Interim history: I reviewed Thomas Reid in clinic today; he attended alone. He reports a challenging exam/professional assessment season with heightened stress, markedly disturbed sleep, and irregular meals. He describes increased post-ictal fatigue, prolonged recovery times after convulsive seizures, and more pronounced cognitive fog affecting study and work performance. He also reports multiple near-falls at home, with one witnessed minor head knock without loss of consciousness, and escalating anxiety regarding unpredictability of episodes. Occupational disruption has increased, with missed deadlines and reduced capacity to commute independently.

Seizure summary: Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal non-motor with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported. In the context of the above, although headline frequency is unchanged, he perceives deterioration due to greater seizure clustering around stress points, longer post-ictal recovery, and increased functional impact on safety, study, and activities of daily living.

Co-morbidities and relevant history: Mild asthma (inhaler PRN). No recent infections. Sleeps 4–5 hours on average during assessments. Caffeine intake increased. No alcohol misuse. Lives with a flatmate; does not currently drive.

Examination (today): Alert and oriented. Speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. No focal neurological deficits. Cardiorespiratory exam normal. No tongue laceration or shoulder tenderness today. Weight stable.

Current medications (patient-reported):
- Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily.
- Perampanel 6 mg at night.
- Folic acid 5 mg daily.
- Salbutamol inhaler PRN.

Adverse effects: Reports intermittent dizziness and word-finding difficulty, more noticeable during the high-stress period. No new rash, no visual blurring, no mood lability beyond situational anxiety.

Safety and psychosocial: Increased falls risk reported. Using a shower chair and avoids baths. Flatmate aware of seizure first aid. He has contacted the university disability support team; exam accommodations pending. He expresses concern about worsening day-to-day coping despite stable numerical seizure counts.

Plan:
- We discussed the interaction between stress, sleep deprivation, and seizure control. He will prioritise regular sleep and meals, with alarms to stabilise lamotrigine timing.
- I have arranged an epilepsy specialist nurse telephone review in 2–3 weeks to reassess support needs during the ongoing assessment period and to reinforce safety planning.
- Request community occupational therapy home safety assessment given reported near-falls and drop attacks.
- Bloods: lamotrigine level, FBC, U&Es, LFTs, vitamin D, and ferritin to check for reversible contributors to fatigue.
- We discussed future options including structured psychological support for anxiety and stress-management strategies, and potential re-referral for video-EEG telemetry if functional impact remains high.
- Rescue plan reaffirmed for prolonged convulsive seizure or cluster, and guidance provided regarding when to seek urgent care.

I will review Thomas Reid in clinic in 3 months, or sooner if there is any escalation in convulsive frequency, injury, or concerns from the patient or carers.

Yours sincerely

Diagnoses: 

Focal epilepsy, likely frontal onset, with nocturnal predominance.

I reviewed Michael Harris in our epilepsy clinic today by scheduled telephone appointment, which he prefers given his frequent international business travel and irregular sleep across time zones. He reports generally good adherence to lamotrigine, with occasional delayed evening doses when flying long-haul. He has been using a mobile app to track time zone changes and dosing times, which appears to have reduced missed doses. He denies adverse effects beyond occasional transient dizziness when sleep-deprived. There is no current use of rescue medication and he has not required emergency services since last year.

Seizure history since the last review: In November he had a run of one seizure within half an hour (not full generalised tonic-clonic, fluctuating in intensity, resolved without medication). In December there was 7 further seizures at night, and in March another during physiotherapy (unwitnessed by mother). He associates the November and December events with multiple red-eye flights, jet lag and fragmented sleep. The March episode followed a strenuous rehabilitation session for a minor ankle injury while he was mildly sleep-deprived; there was no reported trauma, tongue biting or incontinence. Post-ictal confusion, when present, is brief. He continues to describe a reliable focal aware aura of a rising epigastric sensation and brief speech arrest, sometimes progressing to impaired awareness; secondary generalisation has not been reported during this interval.

Examination is not applicable today as this was a telephone consultation. Review of prior investigations: MRI brain (last year) was structurally normal; routine EEG showed intermittent right frontal sharp activity with increased expression during drowsiness. No changes to his cardiac or metabolic profiles have been noted by his GP in recent blood tests. 

Plan: 
- Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily. We discussed setting dual time-zone alarms to anchor dosing to home time for flights exceeding 6 hours, shifting by 1–2 hours per day to avoid large dosing gaps. 
- Provide a written travel seizure plan for airlines and hotel staff; patient to carry a copy on all trips. 
- Sleep hygiene advice emphasised, particularly avoidance of red-eye flights where feasible and limiting overnight work before major time zone changes. 
- No rescue medication indicated at present given self-limited events and absence of generalised tonic–clonic seizures; we will revisit if frequency increases. 
- Arrange ambulatory EEG if nocturnal frequency persists over the next quarter. 
- Follow-up in three months by telephone, or earlier if there is escalation, injury, or emergence of generalised tonic–clonic seizures.

Yours sincerely

Thank you for asking me to review this patient in the neurology clinic. She attended with her partner, who helped provide the history. They describe a clear deterioration over the past two months with limited benefit from the prior medication regimen despite good adherence. In Oct she had no nocturnal seizures but 2 daytime events. In Nov she had a nocturnal seizure and 5 while awake. The daytime episodes were reported as sudden behavioural arrest with loss of awareness, brief right-hand clenching, and a confused, irritable recovery lasting 20–30 minutes. The nocturnal event was witnessed as a cry, stiffening, and subsequent heavy breathing with bite to the lateral tongue and marked fatigue the following day.

Intercurrently, she reports escalating anxiety about further events, fragmented sleep, and difficulty maintaining work attendance. There have been no new neurological focal symptoms reported between events. She denies recent head injury, fever, or intoxication. There is no current pregnancy. She has been fully compliant with her medications; however, she and her partner feel that the overall control is worsening, consistent with the above frequency data.

On examination today, she was alert, oriented, and cooperative. Cranial nerves were intact, tone and power were normal in all limbs, reflexes symmetrical, and plantar responses flexor. Coordination and gait were unremarkable. Blood pressure and heart rate were within normal limits. No cutaneous stigmata were observed.

Investigations to date (summarised from her record and patient report): prior routine EEG reportedly non-diagnostic; MRI brain from last year described as without acute lesion. Serum AED levels from earlier this year suggested therapeutic range, though no recent levels are available. There is no documentation of prolonged EEG monitoring.

Overall impression: the clinical picture is concerning for a deterioration in seizure burden with recent emergence of nocturnal involvement and increasing daytime frequency, in the context of limited benefit from the existing medication regimen. Further characterisation of current events is required to guide management and to assess for potentially modifiable factors.

Plan discussed with the patient and partner:
- Arrange urgent ambulatory/home video EEG monitoring followed by targeted in-patient scalp telemetry if events are captured insufficiently in the community setting.
- Check routine bloods including FBC, U&E, LFTs, bone profile, vitamin D, and trough antiepileptic drug levels; repeat within two weeks.
- Provide seizure first-aid and risk counselling (supervised bathing, cooking precautions, and driving advice as per DVLA guidance given ongoing events).
- Issue seizure diary and advise prompt reporting of any increase in frequency, clustering, or injury.
- Liaise with the epilepsy specialist nurse team for interim support and workplace advice.

I will review her in clinic after the ambulatory EEG, or sooner if there is any further deterioration. Please do not hesitate to contact me if you need additional information.

Yours sincerely

Thank you for your referral and the recent update. I reviewed Daniel Foster in clinic today. He reports a clear improvement in seizure control over the past three months, coinciding with the adoption of a structured remote work arrangement that has reduced commuting-related fatigue and allowed more regular sleep. He and his partner have maintained a seizure diary which shows a more predictable pattern and fewer prolonged events.

He has focal epilepsy. His typical seizures begin with a brief rising epigastric sensation and tingling in the right hand, followed by behavioural arrest and impaired awareness for under a minute, sometimes with right-hand automatisms and brief post-event disorientation. There has been no generalised tonic–clonic activity reported for over six months. No injuries, tongue biting, or incontinence have been described in this interval.

Current pattern: Morning clusters 2×/month; ~2 to 4 events over 90 min. These clusters are usually preceded by a shorter night or early wake for meetings. Outside of these cluster mornings, he has had only two isolated focal impaired-awareness events in the last eight weeks. He attributes the overall improvement to reduced early starts, scheduled rest breaks, and better hydration as part of his remote working routine.

Medication adherence is good with no missed doses reported. He remains on lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily and levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily without adverse effects beyond mild, transient irritability early in treatment, now resolved. No new neurological symptoms, headaches, or visual changes. Mood is stable and he denies anxiety around seizures; Epworth Sleepiness Scale is 6/24. Weight, BP, and basic metabolic profile from last month via the GP were unremarkable according to the shared record summary.

Examination today was normal: alert and orientated, fluent speech, intact cranial nerves, full power in all limbs, normal coordination and gait. No focal deficits were elicited.

Impression
- Focal epilepsy with improving control and reduced burden of prolonged events.
- Residual vulnerability to morning clustering when sleep is curtailed, now less frequent with remote work and a regular schedule.

Plan
- Continue lamotrigine and levetiracetam at current doses.
- Reinforce sleep regularity and morning routine that has correlated with improvement (patient-led measures already effective).

St Mary's Hospital 

Institute of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 March 2017 

Dr Patel  
St Mary's Hospital  
Institute of Neurology, St Mary's Hospital, London W2 1NY  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Amelia Johnson, DOB: 12-05-1990, Hospital No: H584203 NHS No. 5938472610  
14 Brookside Close, London, W10 5LS 

Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily. Patient-reported adherence is generally good with occasional missed evening doses.

History and assessment: 

This patient has focal epilepsy, with semiology of a rising epigastric sensation followed by behavioural arrest and impaired awareness lasting 1–2 minutes, occasionally progressing to right arm posturing. Remote history of mild traumatic brain injury in late adolescence is noted without lasting neurological deficit. Interval reports describe “Seizures with missed ASM doses,” typically occurring within 24–48 hours of a missed levetiracetam dose, most often when evening doses are delayed during shift work. No convulsive events reported in the past year. No new focal neurological symptoms. Sleep disruption and higher caffeine intake during night shifts are described. No alcohol misuse.

Examination (teleclinic history corroborated with prior in-person notes): Alert, oriented, normal speech and language. No reported motor deficits, gait issues, or visual changes. Prior MRI brain (epilepsy protocol, 2019) without clear lesion. Routine EEG (2020) showed intermittent left temporal slowing without definite epileptiform discharges.

Plan: 
- Continue levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily. Given the clear pattern of “Seizures with missed ASM doses,” we discussed practical adherence supports (alarms and blister packs) and the option of shifting the schedule by one hour earlier to better align with night-shift patterns; patient in agreement to trial this. 
- Patient has a valid UK driving licence and is aware of DVLA guidance; she remains seizure-free when doses are taken and will inform us immediately if any episodes occur despite full adherence. 
- Safety advice reiterated regarding bathing and working at height. 
- No immediate need for medication change as seizures correlate with missed doses; if breakthrough events occur despite full adherence, we will consider adjunctive lacosamide or lamotrigine and repeat EEG. 

Follow-up: Review in clinic in six months, sooner if seizures occur despite full adherence.

Yours sincerely

KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Patel
Riverside Medical Practice

Dear Dr Patel
Michael Green, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: G784512 NHS No. 7392018456
12 Beckford Court, Deptford, London, SE8 4NX 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. He remains under long-term follow-up, with antiseizure therapy and bone-health monitoring arranged and recorded elsewhere. Since his last routine review, he reports that his day-to-day function has been stable with regular work attendance and independent living. Sleep is satisfactory though he notes occasional late evenings due to shift patterns.

With regard to events, he describes two distinct patterns that have persisted over the past year. Generalised tonic-clonic seizures are rare, typically 3 events per year. focal sensory occur several times each week, particularly in the evenings. He identifies stress and missed meals as potential precipitants for the focal sensory episodes. There is no consistent diurnal pattern for the convulsions, and no reported injuries. Post-event recovery is prompt after the focal episodes; following convulsions he requires several hours of rest with headache and myalgia the next day. He does not drive and remains aware of DVLA guidance.

Medication adherence is good by pill count and pharmacy records. He reports mild intermittent dizziness in the late afternoon and occasional tingling in the fingertips, without rash or visual disturbance. No recent hospital admissions. Weight has been stable. He continues vitamin D and calcium as per previous plan; bone-density surveillance is ongoing via the metabolic bone clinic.

On examination today: BP 122/74, pulse 72 regular. Neurological examination was unremarkable with normal gait, intact cranial nerves, and no focal deficits. No tremor or nystagmus. Skin clear of rashes.

Investigations: Routine bloods last month showed normal FBC, renal and liver profiles. Vitamin D is within target range on supplementation. Prior EEGs have been non-diagnostic, and MRI brain (reported previously) showed no structural correlate. A repeat ambulatory EEG has been requested to better sample evening events; scheduling is in progress.

Plan:
- Continue current antiseizure regimen without change pending ambulatory EEG results.
- Reinforce regular meals, hydration, and sleep hygiene; he will keep a simple event diary noting timing (especially evenings), potential triggers, and recovery.
- Safety advice reiterated regarding bathing, heights, and operating machinery.
- Coordinate with the metabolic bone team to ensure the next bone-density assessment proceeds as planned; results to be copied to this clinic.
- Follow-up in 4–6 months, or sooner if there is an increase in convulsions, medication intolerance, or injury.

He understands the above and agrees with the plan. I will write again once the ambulatory EEG has been reported.

Yours sincerely

Centre for Epilepsy

Dr Amelia Khan 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy

Dear Dr Amelia Khan, 

Alex Turner, DOB: 22-09-2003, Hospital No: H847520 NHS No. 9041127765  
Flat 2, 14 Brookside Road, London, SW19 3AB  

Diagnosis: 

Focal epilepsy with sleep deprivation and circadian disruption as prominent precipitants; reports seizure events during travel/jet lag 

Aetiology: 

No structural lesion identified on MRI; EEG with left temporal intermittent sharp activity 

Other Medical Problems: 

Intermittent migraines without aura; myopia; no known drug allergies 

Management Plan: 

- Continue Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily, single brand only.  
- Optimise sleep hygiene with consistent bed and wake times; patient acknowledges prolonged evening screen exposure due to university esports leadership role.  
- For long-haul trips and competition travel, implement structured sleep-shift plan and ensure medication timing remains on home schedule when feasible, given the clear pattern of seizure events during travel/jet lag.  
- Rescue plan unchanged: Buccal midazolam 10 mg for any convulsive event >5 minutes; university esports staff informed.  
- DVLA guidance has been discussed and reinforced.  
- Bloods today: FBC, U&E, LFT, Lamotrigine trough level.  
- Review in six months, sooner if breakthrough events occur.  

This student is the lead for the university esports club and frequently manages late-night practices, extended tournaments, and cross-time-zone travel for events. He describes reliable triggers as prolonged screen time into the early hours and abrupt sleep schedule changes. He remains otherwise well, with no adverse effects from Lamotrigine and good adherence. Since our last contact he had two brief focal aware seizures associated with a red-eye flight to North America and a subsequent return journey, consistent with seizure events during travel/jet lag. There have been no injuries or secondary generalisation. We discussed practical adjustments including blue-light filters, scheduled screen breaks, and a pre-travel sleep phase shift. He prefers to maintain Lamotrigine at the current dosing; I agree provided levels are satisfactory and events remain infrequent and clearly precipitated. No additional antiseizure medication is indicated at present. 

Yours sincerely

He is keeping a seizure and trigger diary and has found this helpful alongside the dietary plan. 

On examination today he was well, with normal observations and no new neurological signs. We discussed ongoing lifestyle measures, including maintaining regular sleep, hydration, and adherence to the dietetic plan, as these appear to be contributing to his recent improvement. He prefers to continue with the current approach and monitoring, given the recent stability. 

Plan:
- Continue current regimen and supervised dietetic programme.
- Maintain seizure/trigger diary and sleep routine; family to continue to witness and document events if any recur.
- Safety advice reiterated, including avoidance of sleep deprivation and excess alcohol.
- Routine follow-up in three months, earlier if there is any recurrence or change in pattern. 

Overall, his condition is improving, with no seizures since the first week after discontinuing Levetiracetam and subjective gains in daytime alertness and consistency of routine under dietetic guidance. 

Yours sincerely

Dear  Dr Kate 
 Wendy Brown , DOB:  21-11-1982 , Hospital No:  K482715  NHS No.  6592841037 
 Flat 7 Brookside Avenue, Newcastle-under-Lyme, ST5 2QD  

Diagnoses/summary: Recurrent seizures under review; current working diagnosis remains under investigation with no definitive classification. No clear structural lesion identified to date. Overall clinical stability improving in recent weeks with better adherence.

Previous investigations: Routine MRI brain (2015) reported as normal. Screening EEG (2015) non-diagnostic. No telemetry performed. Bloods including FBC, U&E, LFTs and bone profile within reference ranges (last checked January 2017).

Medication history: Previously trialled levetiracetam (mood irritability), discontinued. Currently on lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily with good tolerability. Uses vitamin D supplementation. No known drug allergies.

Seizure history and current status: The first seizure was reported in December 2016 while visiting relatives in Canada. It happened during an afternoon nap, with an unusual tingling spreading from the right hand to the shoulder. The second and third event took place in February 2017 in the USA, again from sleep, lasting approximately five minutes, with comparable symptoms. Since introducing a pillbox and timed phone reminders in late January, missed doses have markedly reduced and there has been no further event since early February. No injuries, tongue biting, or incontinence reported. Post-event confusion minimal and resolved within minutes. Partner corroborates the description of events arising from sleep.

Interim progress: The patient reports improved day-to-day consistency, fewer auras, and better sleep hygiene. No recent headaches, visual disturbance, or mood decline. Adherence has improved substantially with the pillbox/reminder system and this correlates with clinical stability.

Examination today: Alert and oriented, normal speech and language. Cranial nerves intact. Motor and coordination normal. No focal neurological deficit. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable. BMI 24. Blood pressure 118/72.

Plan:
- Continue lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily. Reinforced adherence; the pillbox/reminder strategy appears effective and is likely contributing to the current improvement.
- Safety advice reiterated: avoid sleep deprivation, limit alcohol, and follow general seizure first-aid guidance. No driving until standard seizure-free criteria are met per DVLA guidance.
- Arrange repeat EEG to look for interictal changes; consider extended sleep-deprived protocol if routine study is non-diagnostic.
- Check lamotrigine trough level and repeat routine bloods in 6–8 weeks for baseline monitoring.
- Provide written self-management plan and seizure diary template; patient agrees to record any further events with timing, triggers, and recovery details.
- Follow-up in 3 months, earlier if recurrence or concerns.

Overall impression: The clinical picture is improving with better medication adherence and routine. Stability has improved since implementing pillbox and reminders, and there have been no further events reported since early February.

Yours sincerely

King's College Hospital

Consultant Neurologist
King's College Hospital
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS

Dear Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist,

Daniel Harris, DOB: 17-03-1986, Hospital No: KCH-9245173 NHS No. 9432081675
12 Brookfield Road, London, SE15 4DA

Thank you for asking me to review your patient. He attended with his partner. They brought smartwatch logs from a fall-detection device that timestamps alerts and may help corroborate the chronology of events. Over the past three months, he and his partner report clusters of events with variable frequency: on steadier stretches he may go a week without any, while during flares he experiences multiple events, with a reported frequency of up to seven in bad weeks. The alerts often occur in the late evening or early morning, and there are occasional daytime alerts at work. He describes a brief prodrome of feeling "detached" or "far away" lasting seconds, sometimes followed by a collapse noted by the watch. Recovery is described as slow on some occasions and quicker on others. He denies clear triggers, though he notes disrupted sleep and high work stress in the days preceding more frequent alerts.

Witness accounts remain inconsistent regarding semiology, and there is limited reliable observation of motor features. He has not noted tongue biting or urinary incontinence. He has had a few episodes of waking with headache and myalgia after a night-time alert. He does not drive. There is no recent head injury, alcohol excess, or recreational drug use reported. Previous antiseizure medication trials were stopped due to either mood change or lethargy; he is currently not taking regular antiseizure therapy.

We discussed a structured approach to further characterisation of his events. He is willing to share the raw smartwatch timestamps and fall alerts so we can compare them with a detailed event diary. He is open to inpatient scalp telemetry if needed, but wished first to try ambulatory monitoring so that we can capture events in his usual environment. I have explained that while the smartwatch alerts are helpful for time-stamping possible events, they are non-specific and cannot in themselves define the nature of the episodes. He and his partner understand this limitation.

Mood-wise, he reports intermittent anxiety around the unpredictability of the episodes and the impact on employment. There is no current history of hallucinations or sustained thought disorder. Sleep is fragmented; he is trialling earlier bedtimes and reducing late caffeine. He remains keen to coordinate with occupational health regarding workplace safety.

Plan agreed with the patient:
- Correlate smartwatch fall-detection timestamps with a contemporaneous event diary for 8–10 weeks.
- Arrange ambulatory EEG with ECG to capture events at home; escalate to inpatient scalp telemetry if ambulatory study is non-diagnostic and events continue.
- Basic bloods including FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile, thyroid function, B12/folate, and vitamin D as part of general health review.
- Sleep hygiene measures and stress-management support; provide information on patient charities for practical advice.

I will review him in clinic after the ambulatory study or sooner if there is any marked deterioration. Please let me know if you would like any further information.

Yours sincerely

Dear Dr Priya Shah
Consultant Neurologist,  

Emma Carter, DOB: 14-02-1989, Hospital No: KCH728561 NHS No. 9463821750  
12 Brookfield Road, London, SE15 3GX

Thank you for asking me to review Emma Carter in the epilepsy clinic. She attended with her partner. They brought a printout from a menstrual tracking app and a diary of events since spring. The history suggests a possible cyclical influence on seizure occurrence, with several clusters aligning with the late luteal phase according to the app’s logged cycles. She does not identify a consistent aura, and recovery times vary from minutes to an hour depending on the event.

To summarise the recent pattern from her diary and the centre’s incident reports: In May he had 3 short seizures in a brief series. In Aug 7 daytime events were noted, in Sep four myoclonic jerks occurred, and in Oct five generalised tonic-clonic seizures at the centre (staff-witnessed). The patient and partner note that the May and August clusters coincided with days −3 to +2 around menses on the app logs, while September was peri-ovulatory by their record, and October overlapped with the late luteal phase again. There is no clear precipitant beyond possible hormonal timing, though poor sleep was recorded before two of the October events.

Background features: onset in late adolescence; no history of febrile seizures, CNS infection, or head injury. Family history negative for seizures. Medications: currently on lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily and levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily. Adherence is reported as good; no recent dose changes. Adverse effects include intermittent irritability and mild insomnia. She does not drive. No alcohol or recreational drugs. Neurological examination today was unremarkable.

Prior investigations: MRI brain (2016) reported normal. Routine EEG (2016) showed no definite epileptiform discharges. Thyroid function and general biochemistry (May 2017) were within reference limits. Vitamin D previously low-normal; replaced by GP.

Plan: 
- Continue present anti-seizure medications without change for now pending correlation work-up. 
- Arrange ambulatory EEG with event-marking to sample over two full cycles if feasible; we will align scheduling with the app-predicted late luteal window first. 
- Request serum lamotrigine and levetiracetam trough levels next week and again in the late luteal phase to assess any pharmacokinetic variability. 
- Provide seizure first-aid reinforcement to the patient and workplace; centre staff reports have been helpful and will continue. 
- Encourage continued detailed diary entries, including cycle dates, sleep duration, and potential stressors. The patient has opted to share app screenshots with our team monthly. 
- Safety: discussed SUDEP risk factors, adherence, sleep regularity, and supervision during bathing; she has a shower chair and avoids swimming alone. 

I will review her after the ambulatory EEG and drug levels are available, ideally within 8–10 weeks. If the cycle linkage is substantiated, we can discuss targeted strategies around vulnerable days. She and her partner are in agreement with this approach.

Please let me know if there is additional workplace documentation you would like me to provide. Thank you for the referral.

Diagnoses: 

Focal epilepsy, likely left temporal onset, with secondary generalisation risk. 

History and current status: 

I reviewed Emily Rogers in clinic today. She works as a warehouse picker operating between tall racking and narrow aisles, where lifting tasks and the work pace vary by shift. She reports that her typical event begins with a brief rising epigastric sensation and déjà vu lasting 15–30 seconds, occasionally followed by impaired awareness with automatisms and a short post-event confusion. She has not required rescue medication in the past 12 months and there have been no injuries at work. 

Regarding seizure frequency, she describes a fluctuating pattern around her shift rota. On occasions she is seizure-free for 4 consecutive days, followed by a day with multiple events, typically two tonic seizures. She links these cluster days to early-start shifts with higher lifting demand, though she is unsure whether sleep disruption or physical load is the main trigger. There has been no recent change in caffeine or alcohol intake, and she denies intercurrent illness. 

Medication and investigations: 

- Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily. She reports full adherence and no dose omissions during shift changes.  
- Levetiracetam 500 mg morning, 1,000 mg evening. She notes mild irritability on stressful days but no mood change warranting intervention.  
- Last MRI head (3T, epilepsy protocol) 18 months ago: non-lesional.  
- Last routine EEG 12 months ago: left temporal intermittent rhythmic delta activity with rare sharp waves.  

Examination today was unremarkable with normal neurological findings. No injuries or tongue bites reported since last review. 

Workplace context and safety: 

She reports narrow-aisle navigation with handheld scanners and intermittent ladder use to shoulder height; powered lifting aids are available for heavier items. She states she avoids working at height and does not operate forklifts. She also indicates that supervisors are aware of her diagnosis and that a colleague can be called promptly if she feels an aura. 

Plan: 

- Continue current anti-seizure medications without change.  
- Arrange ambulatory EEG to capture events during a representative workday.  
- Bloods today: lamotrigine and levetiracetam trough levels, U&E, LFT, FBC, vitamin D.  
- Provide updated seizure action information for her employer, confirming she does not operate vehicles or work at unguarded height; patient reports this already aligns with her current duties.  
- Seizure diary to correlate events with shift type, sleep duration, and lifting load.  
- Review in clinic in four months or sooner if frequency increases or injuries occur. 

Yours sincerely

Department of Neurology

Dr Amelia Patel  
St George’s Hospital

Dear Dr Amelia Patel,  

Michael Turner, DOB: 22-03-1988, Hospital No: H473920 NHS No. 9938475612  
18 Willow Lane, Wimbledon, London SW19 2PA 

Diagnoses:  

Two unprovoked seizures under evaluation; EEG pending (booked July 2015).  

Current Medication:  

No regular antiepileptic medication at present. PRN buccal midazolam not prescribed.  

Plan:  

- Arrange routine EEG and brain MRI with epilepsy protocol.  
- Provide written seizure first-aid and SUDEP risk counselling.  
- Discuss driving regulations and notify DVLA as appropriate.  
- Review in clinic after investigations; earlier if further events occur.  
- Liaise with community pharmacy regarding continuity of supply if treatment is initiated.  

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today following two recent events suggestive of seizures. The first seizure was reported in April 2015 while visiting relatives in Canada. It happened during an afternoon nap, with an unusual tingling spreading from the right hand to the shoulder. The second event took place in June 2015 in the USA, again from sleep, lasting approximately five minutes, with comparable symptoms. There was no clear precipitant identified. There is no personal history of significant head injury, CNS infection, or febrile convulsions. Family history is non-contributory by the patient’s account. Alcohol intake is modest and there is no recreational drug use reported. Sleep had been irregular around the times of travel.  

Past variability in pharmacy supply is documented in the records from previous long-term prescriptions for non-neurological conditions; the patient reports that adherence was impacted at times when pharmacies substituted brands or experienced stock interruptions. We discussed the importance of consistency of supply should an antiseizure medicine be commenced, and I will ensure any future prescription specifies brand consistency and that the community pharmacy is briefed to minimise switching.  

On examination today, general and neurological examinations were normal. Observations were stable. There are no lateralising signs.  

Given the limited number of events, absence of ongoing neurological deficit, and pending investigations, we agreed not to start an antiseizure medication today. Should there be any recurrence, especially with evolving features, we will reconsider treatment promptly. The patient has been advised on sleep hygiene, avoidance of sleep deprivation, and to keep a clear record of any further episodes including timing, duration, semiology, and potential triggers.  

I have advised that they must not drive and should follow DVLA guidance for a first unprovoked seizure, with notification to the DVLA; I have provided supporting documentation. Workplace safety and water safety precautions were reviewed. A seizure action plan and emergency guidance have been supplied.  

I will see Michael Turner after the EEG and MRI are completed, or sooner if further events occur. Please let me know if there are questions or if additional information from primary care becomes available.  

With best wishes,

I reviewed Emily Carter in clinic today. She reports a longstanding history of episodic events, with a notable previous seizure occurring during air travel; she describes significant anticipatory anxiety around flying since then. There is no clear precipitant beyond sleep disruption and travel-related stress, and there are no consistent focal warnings reported. 

Over the past year seizure control has been relatively stable. She experiences two generalised tonic-clonic seizures every 2 months. Absence seizures remain infrequent, usually no more than twice weekly, and myoclonic jerks are reported only occasionally in the mornings. She feels Levetiracetam has been better tolerated overall, with occasional irritability that she manages through exercise and structured routines. 

We discussed strategies to mitigate travel-related triggers including sleep prioritisation, hydration, avoiding alcohol, and using aisle seats to reduce stress. She expresses interest in graded exposure and psychological support to address her travel anxiety, which I believe is appropriate.

King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 12 May 2019 

Dr Helen Carter
Consultant Neurologist

Dear Dr Helen Carter
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Everett, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: K742119 NHS No. 9075314682  
12 Brook View, Herne Hill, London SE24 0AB

Thank you for referring this patient for review following several recent events suggestive of ongoing seizure activity. I met with him and his partner in clinic today. He reports a remote history of a mild traumatic brain injury in adolescence without documented loss of consciousness longer than a few minutes and no known neurosurgical intervention at the time. There is no family history of seizures to his knowledge. He does not drive.

Over the past four months there has been an increase in events described by the patient and corroborated by his partner. In Feb two generalised tonic-clonic seizures were reported. In Mar a nocturnal seizure occurred, and in Apr another during respite care. The daytime events were said to begin without a consistent warning; witnesses describe bilateral stiffening and rhythmic jerks lasting approximately 90–120 seconds with a bitten lateral tongue on one occasion and post-event confusion for 30–45 minutes. The nocturnal event was inferred from a bed partner report of witnessed jerking, vocalisation, and subsequent morning myalgia, tongue soreness, and headache. The episode during respite care was recorded in the facility log with similar semiology and a comparable period of post-event disorientation.

Between events he reports intermittent fatigue and reduced concentration but no clear focal neurological symptoms. He is currently taking levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily as his sole antiseizure medicine, which he started eight months ago; adherence is reported as good with a single missed evening dose in late March. He denies alcohol excess or recreational drug use. Sleep has been fragmented since February owing to anxiety about further events. There is no history provided to suggest consistent triggers.

On examination today he was alert and oriented with fluent speech. Cranial nerves were intact, power 5/5 in all limbs, tone normal, and coordination and gait without focal abnormality. Fundoscopy was unremarkable. No cutaneous stigmata were present. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations were normal. There were no features to suggest an intercurrent infection.

Prior investigations: a routine EEG performed two years ago was reported as within normal limits. A CT head following an event in 2017 showed no acute abnormality; there is no prior MRI available for review. Routine blood tests last month (FBC, U&E, LFT, glucose, CRP) were within reference ranges; magnesium was not checked.

Impression: The clinical description is of recurrent convulsive events with post-event confusion and a probable nocturnal event. The overall pattern remains difficult to classify on clinical grounds alone, and the contribution of the remote mild traumatic brain injury is uncertain. The recent clustering across February to April warrants further evaluation and optimisation of management.

Plan discussed with the patient and partner:
- Arrange prolonged ambulatory EEG with sleep capture to characterise events, and request MRI brain with an epilepsy protocol given the absence of prior MRI.
- Repeat routine bloods including magnesium and vitamin D; check levetiracetam trough level for adherence context.
- Provide seizure first-aid advice and written information; partner is comfortable administering lateral positioning and timing events. Rescue midazolam was not deemed necessary today pending further characterisation, but we will revisit after monitoring if events escalate.
- Reinforce safety guidance: avoid heights, unsupervised swimming, and operate machinery with caution. Driving restrictions as per DVLA Group 1 apply; he states he is not currently driving and understands the need to inform the DVLA and his insurer.
- Sleep hygiene advice offered; patient-reported anxiety will be reviewed after investigations and he is open to brief psychological support if needed.

We will review in clinic once investigations are completed, or sooner if there is any deterioration. Please let me know if you would like any additional tests arranged locally.

Yours sincerely

Cholecalciferol 1000 units once daily.  
Omeprazole 20 mg once daily.  

I reviewed William Harris in clinic today by video, with his partner present. He reports meaningful improvement over the past six weeks. He has been using a pillbox with phone reminders, which has markedly reduced missed doses. Since adopting this routine, he describes better day-to-day steadiness, fewer lapses in attention, and improved sleep regularity. He is back to light exercise and short walks without unsteadiness, and is keeping a simple diary that shows consistent dosing. He has returned to part-time work from home and feels his confidence is increasing. 

Seizures: His initial event was in October 2020 in Germany, arising from sleep. He awoke with jerking of the left arm and facial twitching. A second event occurred in Italy the following December 2020, once more during the night, lasting four minutes with similar semiology. There have been no further events since initiation of regular dosing and use of the pillbox/reminder system. He denies any daytime episodes, tongue biting, or injuries. His partner has not observed abnormal movements since mid-December. 

Investigations to date: Routine bloods in November 2020 were unremarkable including FBC, U&E, LFTs; vitamin D low-normal now on supplementation. An outpatient MRI brain (non-contrast) reported no acute or structural abnormalities. A standard EEG in December 2020 was normal in wakefulness and drowsiness. 

Side effects/adherence: He notes transient irritability on starting levetiracetam which has settled. No somnolence or dizziness currently. He has not missed doses since using the pillbox and reminders. 

Risk and lifestyle: Sleep routine improved; alcohol abstinent; no recreational drugs. He does not drive and understands DVLA requirements. Workplace has adjusted hours to support consistent sleep. 

Plan:  
- Continue levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily with ongoing adherence supports (pillbox and reminders).  
- Provide seizure and medication diary template; partner to continue observations during sleep.  
- Safety advice around sleep and bathing reiterated; avoid sleep deprivation.  
- Recheck vitamin D in primary care in 3 months.  
- No further investigations at present; will consider ambulatory EEG only if events recur.  
- Follow-up neurology review in 4 months, sooner if any recurrence or adverse effects. 

Overall, his condition is improving with stable medication adherence and better daily stability since introducing the pillbox and reminders. 

Yours sincerely

Diagnoses/summary: Focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset, drug-resistant; history of mixed seizure types with nocturnal predominance; cumulative fatigue with perceived worsening in the context of irregular shift work in a care setting with frequent patient transfers.

Imaging/EEG: MRI 2017 reported no definite epileptogenic lesion. Prior routine EEGs with intermittent left temporal sharp transients; no definitive lateralising lesion captured on brief recordings.

Previous medication: Lamotrigine (ongoing), Levetiracetam (behavioural irritability), Carbamazepine (dizziness, hyponatraemia), Sodium Valproate (weight gain, tremor), Clobazam (sedation), Lacosamide (no clear benefit), Topiramate (word-finding difficulty), Zonisamide (renal colic), Perampanel (mood change), Pregabalin (somnolence), Rufinamide (GI upset). Vagus nerve stimulation discussed in past but not pursued.

Current medication: Lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily; Midazolam 10 mg buccal PRN for prolonged convulsive seizures; Vitamin D supplementation.

I reviewed Daniel Harris in clinic today, accompanied by his father. He works in a residential care home where breaks are irregular and he is often involved in frequent patient transfers. He reports accumulating fatigue across the week with poorer sleep consolidation. He and his parents have noticed increased recovery time after seizures, more bruising from sudden drops, and reduced confidence mobilising unaccompanied. Although his seizures fluctuate, his parents consider him reasonably well controlled, he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 2 - 3 nights per week, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic seizures during both day and night, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change. However, functionally he is deteriorating: he is missing more work days, requires more supervision at home, and has described worsening morning confusion and headaches consistent with sleep fragmentation. He denies new infectious triggers or recent head injury. No alcohol or recreational drugs. He uses a smartphone seizure diary which corroborates clustered drops particularly after late shifts.

Examination today: Alert and orientated. Speech fluent. No focal neurological deficit. No tongue laceration currently; healing ecchymosis over right forearm consistent with recent drop. Blood pressure 122/74 mmHg, pulse 72 bpm regular. BMI 24.1 kg/m2.

Risk/safety: He reports two nocturnal convulsions this past week without supervision as his parents were away one night; he used his bed sensor alarm on the second occasion. No status epilepticus. No recent aspiration, but he describes biting inside cheek. No known pregnancy considerations. Driving: he is not driving and understands ongoing DVLA restrictions.

Work and function: Care setting duties with manual handling appear to aggravate fatigue and perceived seizure burden. We discussed reasonable adjustments at work (regular protected breaks, reduced overnight duties, avoiding lone working for transfers) and he will raise this with occupational health.

Plan:
- Continue Lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily for now; adherence good. We discussed options including revisiting VNS and consideration of inpatient video-EEG telemetry to clarify seizure semiology and assess candidacy for neuromodulation. He is open to VNS; I have provided written information and will refer to the device clinic.
- Arrange 5–7 day home sleep study and actigraphy via community sleep service to quantify sleep fragmentation, given nocturnal predominance and daytime fatigue.
- Bloods: U&Es, LFTs, bone profile, vitamin D, FBC, lamotrigine trough level, and HbA1c (rule out metabolic contributors to fatigue).
- Safety: reinforce use of bed rails as appropriate, padded headboard, avoid baths unsupervised, ensure buccal midazolam is in-date and that family trained; employer to review manual handling plan.
- Occupational health letter to support phased adjustments including structured breaks and minimising consecutive late shifts.
- Seizure diary: continue electronic log with tags for shift pattern, sleep duration, perceived stress, and meal times to correlate with clusters.
- Follow-up: telephone review by epilepsy nurse specialist in 4 weeks to reassess clusters and injuries; clinic review with me in 3 months or earlier if further deterioration (injuries, prolonged convulsions, status, or intolerable side-effects).

I emphasise that while the absolute seizure pattern appears long-standing, his functional trajectory is deteriorating with cumulative fatigue, more injuries from drop attacks, and increasing post-ictal recovery time in the context of irregular work patterns. We will proceed with the above investigations and VNS pathway discussion to mitigate further decline.

Yours sincerely

I will review Michael Harris in six weeks or sooner if there are further prolonged events, head injuries, or any concern about increased frequency beyond current patterns. Yours sincerely

12 Fernbrook Gardens, London, SE24 8QJ

Thank you for referring this patient for neurological review. I met him today in clinic accompanied by his partner. He described longstanding episodes that he and his family have termed “spells,” beginning in his late teens. The events are stereotyped: he experiences a brief warning of a rising internal tension and a sense of detachment, followed by behavioural arrest with fixed gaze and lip chewing reported by witnesses. On some occasions there is right arm stiffening and a brief cry. He is typically confused for 15–30 minutes afterwards and complains of a throbbing headache with pronounced fatigue.

Regarding periodicity, he and his partner report that the events tend to group together over several days in a repeating pattern roughly every four to five weeks. Within these burst periods the number of episodes varies and has not been reliably logged; outside these periods he may go a week or two without events. This “regular bursts every few weeks; number during bursts not well documented” pattern has been their usual experience for years. He started levetiracetam six months ago after trials of lamotrigine and carbamazepine were discontinued for rash and dizziness respectively. Notably, since commencing current therapy the overall pattern has persisted without clear change in frequency or recovery time.

There is no consistent relationship with sleep deprivation or alcohol, though he suspects that high work stress and missed meals may act as triggers. No tongue laceration or incontinence has been witnessed. He has had two emergency attendances in the last year for cluster days with prolonged confusion; bloods at those times were unremarkable and a CT head performed three years ago was reported normal. He has never had status epilepticus or significant injury from these events. Family history is non-contributory. He does not drive and is aware of DVLA guidance as previously discussed with his GP.

On examination today he was alert and oriented with normal speech, cranial nerves intact, no focal motor deficit, and normal coordination and gait. Cardiovascular and general examination were unremarkable. There were no features of ongoing post-event confusion at the time of review. Blood pressure 126/78 mmHg, pulse 72 bpm regular.

We discussed next steps. The main issue at present is to clarify the nature of the events and obtain objective correlation. He is agreeable to an ambulatory EEG with event diary, followed by consideration of inpatient video-EEG if the ambulatory study does not capture typical episodes. I have also recommended structured documentation using a calendar or smartphone app to record the timing of warning symptoms, event duration, and recovery features, so that we can quantify numbers during the clustered periods. He will continue levetiracetam at his current dose as he reports tolerability but no perceived benefit so far; we will not change treatment until we have more diagnostic information. I have requested routine bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, vitamin D) to be done at his GP surgery for baseline monitoring.

For safety, we reinforced standard advice around adequate sleep, regular meals, moderated alcohol intake, and seeking urgent help if any event lasts more than five minutes, occurs back-to-back without recovery, or if he sustains significant injury. He understands not to swim unsupervised, to avoid heights, and to take showers instead of baths.

I will review him in clinic after the ambulatory EEG. Thank you again for the referral and for your ongoing care. Please let me know if there is any additional history or prior imaging that would be helpful to obtain before telemetry.

Yours sincerely
